summary: Disables the local Fast Startup setting by writing HiberbootEnabled under HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Session Manager\Power. This is the documented local on/off surface for Fast Startup. A separate policy value, HKLM\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\System\HiberbootEnabled, only forces Fast Startup on and does not provide a symmetric disable path. The app previously wrote HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Power\HiberbootEnabled, which is not the authoritative local Fast Startup toggle; both app surfaces now align to the Session Manager path.

setting.name: Fast Startup (Hiberboot)
setting.casual_explanation: Fast Startup stores part of the system state during shutdown so the next boot can be faster. Some people disable it to avoid dual-boot, driver, or wake issues.
setting.why_people_change_it: People turn it off when they want cleaner full shutdown behavior, better compatibility with dual-boot setups, or less weirdness around hibernation-related resume behavior.
setting.tradeoffs_overview: Turning Fast Startup off can solve compatibility problems, but boot may take longer on some systems. On systems where startup speed matters more than shutdown correctness, leaving it enabled can be preferable.
setting.professional_notes: Official Microsoft guidance documents the local Fast Startup switch at HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Session Manager\Power\HiberbootEnabled, where 0 disables Fast Startup and 1 enables it. The separate WinInit policy HiberbootEnabled under HKLM\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\System is not a disable surface: if that policy is disabled or not configured, Windows uses the local setting instead. nohuto reverse-engineering agrees that PopReadHiberbootGroupPolicy (policy path) overrides PopReadHiberbootPolicy (Session Manager path). On the validation host, HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Power\HiberbootEnabled=0 while HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Session Manager\Power\HiberbootEnabled=1, reinforcing that the Control\Power value is not the authoritative local Fast Startup toggle.

allowed_values.label: Windows managed default
allowed_values.meaning: Windows chooses the Fast Startup baseline based on OS configuration and hibernation capability.

targets.notes: Do not confuse the local Fast Startup toggle with the separate WinInit policy HiberbootEnabled under HKLM\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\System. The policy can force Fast Startup on, but disabled or not configured falls back to the local setting.

app_current_implementation.notes: The app provider and engine helper both write HiberbootEnabled=0 under HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Session Manager\Power. This matches the documented local Fast Startup setting. The previously used Control\Power path was corrected on 2026-03-13.

windows_defaults.label: Windows baseline is feature dependent
windows_defaults.applies_to: Windows devices where hibernation and Fast Startup are available

states.rationale: Fast Startup depends on hibernation support and system configuration.

windows_defaults.notes: Fast Startup baseline remains feature dependent because hibernation support and platform configuration can change whether the setting is available.

recommended_profiles.label: Windows default
intended_for: Systems where Fast Startup has not caused issues
avoid_for: People who need a guaranteed full shutdown behavior

states.rationale: Use Windows baseline when Fast Startup has not caused a known problem.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You want to stay with Windows behavior.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You already know Fast Startup is causing a specific compatibility problem.
tradeoffs: Boot may remain faster than a full shutdown-based startup.

recommended_profiles.label: Troubleshooting and dual boot
intended_for: People debugging shutdown or wake issues
avoid_for: People optimizing only for fastest boot times

states.rationale: Disable Fast Startup to prefer full shutdown behavior over faster boot.

recommended_profiles.good_when: Fast Startup is suspected to be part of the issue.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You mainly care about the shortest possible boot time and have no Fast Startup-related issues.
tradeoffs: Shutdown and next-boot behavior becomes more like a full shutdown cycle.
recommended_profiles.notes: Validated local Fast Startup disable profile. Uses the documented Session Manager Power path, not the WinInit force-enable policy path.

evidence.title: Microsoft Fast Startup guidance for HiberbootEnabled
evidence.summary: Official Microsoft guidance that references HiberbootEnabled under the Session Manager Power path and documents 0 as disabled and 1 as enabled.

evidence.title: Policy CSP - ADMX_WinInit
evidence.summary: Microsoft Learn policy reference for the WinInit Hiberboot policy. It confirms the policy path HKLM\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\System\HiberbootEnabled with enabledValue=1 and disabledValue=0, and states that disabled or not configured falls back to the local setting.

evidence.title: nohuto power asset for HiberbootEnabled research
evidence.summary: Reverse-engineered notes showing that PopReadHiberbootGroupPolicy reads the policy path and overrides PopReadHiberbootPolicy, which reads HiberbootEnabled from Control\Session Manager\Power.

evidence.title: Live build observation of diverging HiberbootEnabled values
evidence.summary: On the validation host, HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Power\HiberbootEnabled = 0 while HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Session Manager\Power\HiberbootEnabled = 1. This shows the Control\Power value can diverge from the documented local Fast Startup toggle.

evidence.title: Current app implementation
evidence.summary: The app provider and engine helper both write HiberbootEnabled=0 under HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Session Manager\Power.

decision.why: The local Fast Startup toggle is explicitly documented by Microsoft at HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Session Manager\Power\HiberbootEnabled, with 0 meaning disabled and 1 meaning enabled. The separate WinInit policy path only forces Fast Startup on and falls back to the local setting when disabled or not configured. The app provider and engine helper now both write the documented local surface, so the implementation matches the researched behavior.

validation_proof.exact_quote_or_path: To disable fast startup, set the following registry value: Key: HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Session Manager\Power Name: HiberbootEnabled Type: DWORD Value: 0 (0 = Disabled, 1 = Enabled)
validation_proof.notes: Official Microsoft guidance documents Fast Startup through HiberbootEnabled under the Session Manager Power path, and the current app now writes that documented local surface.
